digest.top_line: CAN-2409 + definitive 78Gy RT: DFS HR 0.7 (p=0.0155), 2yr biopsy pCR 80 vs 64%; a local-control gain without dose escalation or ADT, OS/PCSM still immature.
digest.tldr: Prostate carried the RT news, and the recurring lesson is that intensifying on top of definitive RT mostly fails hard endpoints: ENZARAD (enza add-on) and COMPPARE (proton vs IMRT) both null, while CAN-2409 bought local control but not yet OS. Outside RT, RASolute 302 hit mOS 13.2 vs 6.6mo (HR 0.40) in 2L pancreatic.

sites.intro: Four prostate reads probing intensification on definitive RT: enzalutamide and protons added nothing on hard endpoints, an oncolytic virus moved only local control, and PROTEUS stays an unreadable preview.

studies.name: COMPPARE
studies.tldr: No proton advantage over IMRT: bowel urgency 5.7 vs 6%, GI G2+ tox 5.2 vs 5.6%, 3yr biochemical control 98.0 vs 97.9%, all NS.
details: 🔍 Prospective comparative-effectiveness cohort (non-randomized), PCORI-funded; 51 centers, 2524 accrued Jul 2018–Oct 2022
🔍 Cohorts: 1500 proton vs 1000 photon; all de novo localized prostate excl very-high-risk and metastatic
⚠️ Realized GI/bowel toxicity ~5-6% in both arms, far below the 15-29% hypothesized for IMRT
⚠️ Low realized event rates leave the comparison likely underpowered to detect a true proton benefit
📊 Rectal spacer cut 2yr GI G2+ toxicity more than modality choice (p=0.009); proton vs IMRT negligible within spacer strata
🔗 vs PARTIQoL (randomized proton vs IMRT, localized prostate): concordant null for patient-reported bowel/GI endpoints
⚠️ Early results; late GI/GU toxicity (the main proton rationale) and long-term control not yet mature
⚠️ Non-randomized cohorts: baseline differences between proton and IMRT pts may confound the read

verdict.rationale: Large multi-site prospective comparative cohort (non-randomized); 3yr null concords with randomized PARTIQoL that protons don't reduce GI toxicity vs IMRT. Late-toxicity follow-up still immature.
verdict.audience: De novo localized prostate cancer, excl very-high-risk and metastatic

studies.significance: Not proton vs photon but the rectal spacer is the toxicity lever: it cut 2yr GI G2+ toxicity (4.4% IMRT, 4.7% proton with spacer vs 7.2% and 8.7% without, p=0.009), dwarfing modality. Both arms fell far below hypothesized rates, so paying the proton premium for less GI toxicity isn't supported.
open_questions: Late GI/GU toxicity beyond 3 years for proton vs IMRT
Long-term biochemical and disease control with longer follow-up

The investigators propose to employ a hypofractionated strategy with our image guided treatment to further improve cancer control and decrease toxicity.

studies.name: CAN-2409
studies.tldr: DFS HR 0.7 (0.52-0.94, p=0.0155), median NR vs 86.1mo, adding intra-prostatic oncolytic virus to definitive 78Gy RT; OS/PCSM immature.
details: 🔍 Intra-prostatic oncolytic-viral therapy (CAN-2409) added to definitive EBRT 78Gy, vs RT + placebo, randomized
⚠️ DFS driven mainly by 2yr post-RT biopsy pCR, a surrogate endpoint
⚠️ OS and PCSM still flat, so clinical benefit unproven
⚠️ Per discussant: control RT 78Gy lacked modern dose intensification (brachy boost, SIB) or ADT, blunting relevance to current SOC
🔗 Local-control gain reached without dose escalation or ADT, a different lever than brachy boost or hormones
📊 ≥Gr 3 toxicity <1% with CAN-2409 added, no major added RT toxicity

verdict.rationale: DFS driven by surrogate 2yr biopsy pCR with flat OS/PCSM; control RT lacks modern dose escalation or ADT, so the divergence isn't yet reliable.

studies.significance: The RT-relevant read is local control: 2yr positive-biopsy fell to 19.6% vs 36.4% (p=0.0015) without dose escalation or added ADT, a new local-intensification lever. But the control RT (78Gy, no boost or ADT) predates modern intensification, leaving its place vs brachy boost or ADT unsettled.
open_questions: Does the DFS benefit translate to OS/PCSM with longer follow-up?
Does benefit hold against modern dose-escalated RT and ADT?

related_trials.brief_title: Phase 3 Study of ProstAtak® Immunotherapy With Standard Radiation Therapy for Localized Prostate Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the effectiveness of ProstAtak® immunotherapy in combination with radiation therapy for patients with intermediate-high risk localized prostate cancer. ProstAtak kills tumor cells and stimulates a cancer vaccine effect. Killing tumor cells in an immune stimulatory environment induces the body's immune system to detect and destroy cancer cells. ProstAtak has shown synergy with radiation without added toxicity and lower than expected recurrence rates in…

* Localized prostate cancer meeting the NCCN criteria of Intermediate Risk or patients having only one NCCN high-risk feature

  * High Risk with a…

studies.name: PROTEUS
studies.tldr: Preview only; most distant mets found by PSMA PET (53.0% apalutamide vs 60.7% ADT), so MFS events were PET- not conventionally-driven.
details: 📊 Preview release; primary MFS effect size (HR, medians) not reported in source tweets.
📊 Distant mets identified by PSMA PET: 53.0% apalutamide vs 60.7% ADT.
⚠️ Majority of MFS events were PET-detected, not conventional imaging, per the NEJM report.
⚠️ More-sensitive PSMA PET ascertainment shifts MFS event counts vs conventional-imaging-era trials.
🔍 Arms per source: apalutamide + ADT vs ADT alone; endpoints reference EFS, BCR, MFS.
🔍 Phase, N, and study population/setting not stated in source tweets.
⚠️ @seanmmcbride 50 vs 60 BCR/100 figures are a hypothetical thought experiment, not reported results.
⚠️ Curator flags as controversial pre-presentation: framed as either homerun or large negative.

verdict.rationale: Preview only; primary MFS magnitude not in source. Lone disclosed detail (most mets PSMA-PET-detected, 53.0% vs 60.7%) raises endpoint-ascertainment concern but can't classify the result.

open_questions: Does the MFS benefit hold when limited to conventional-imaging mets?
Will PSMA-PET-defined MFS translate to an OS benefit?

sites.intro: Upfront RT folded into 1L systemic + PD1 for unresectable MSS met rectal ca, but the single-arm design leaves RT's contribution unmeasurable.

studies.name: MIRACLE-2
studies.tldr: ORR 68%, mOS 23.2mo, 18% reached NED with upfront RT then systemic + PD1 in 1L unresectable MSS met rectal ca.
details: 📊 18% (9/50) reached NED; 1-yr OS 93.3%, 1-yr PFS 33.4%, 1-yr DOR 20%
🔍 Prospective single-arm phase I, N=50, 1L MSS unresectable met rectal ca, primary ≤10cm from anal verge
🔍 RT given upfront: HFRT to primary, HFRT or SBRT to mets. Dose/fractionation not reported in source
💊 Then FOLFOX-bev-tislelizumab (RAS/BRAF-mut) or FOLFIRI-cetux-tislelizumab (WT); RAS/BRAF mut in 56.0%
⚠️ RT-associated lymphopenia a plausible driver of the 36.7% G3/4 lymphopenia
⚠️ RT contribution unmeasurable: no RT-omission arm, effect confounded with systemic + PD1
⚠️ 1° EP is ETS (≥20% shrinkage at 8wk), an early surrogate not OS/PFS; mFU only 19.9mo (16.4-23.4)
⚠️ Durability thin: 1-yr DOR 20%, mDOR 8.0mo despite high 1-yr OS
🔗 MSS ~95% of mCRC and intrinsically IO-resistant; single-agent PD1 inactive in MSS

verdict.rationale: Single-arm phase I, N=50, no randomised RT-omission comparator; primary endpoint is early tumor shrinkage surrogate (ETS), short follow-up. RT effect inseparable from systemic + PD1.
verdict.audience: 1L MSS unresectable metastatic rectal ca, synchronous liver/lung mets

studies.significance: The RT read is upfront priming: HFRT to primary plus HFRT/SBRT to mets precedes systemic + PD1, and 18% (9/50) of unresectable pts reached NED, an unusual signal in immune-cold MSS. RT dose/fractionation isn't in source and no omission arm isolates its effect, so adding RT to a systemic backbone stays hypothesis-only.
open_questions: Does RT add benefit over systemic + PD1 alone (no control arm)?
Biomarkers selecting MSS responders to trimodal therapy
Durability beyond short follow-up (1-yr DOR only 20%)

sites.intro: A systemic standout with no RT relevance: daraxonrasib nearly doubled OS in 2L RAS-G12 metastatic pancreatic.

studies.name: RASolute 302
studies.tldr: mOS 13.2 vs 6.6mo, HR 0.40 (0.30-0.54), P<0.001 for daraxonrasib vs chemo in RAS G12 2L metastatic pancreatic cancer.
details: 🔍 Randomized 2L metastatic PDAC: daraxonrasib vs investigator's-choice chemotherapy
🔍 'Overall' population = RAS G12/G13/Q61-mutant or no RAS mutation detected; G12 the enriched cohort
📊 Deaths 32% daraxonrasib vs 55% chemo in the RAS G12 cohort
⚠️ Daraxonrasib mOS upper CI not reached (10.0-NR); OS data still maturing
⚠️ Investigator's-choice comparator pools heterogeneous 2L regimens; no uniform control arm
🔗 An OS benefit of this magnitude in 2L metastatic PDAC is highly unusual for a historically chemo-refractory setting

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3 with OS primary endpoint hit vs investigator's-choice chemo; HR 0.40 magnitude in dismal-prognosis 2L PDAC clears the bar despite still-maturing follow-up.
verdict.audience: Previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer (RAS G12-mutant cohort)

open_questions: Benefit in non-G12 RAS and RAS-wildtype subgroups
Mature OS once daraxonrasib arm events accrue

bookmarks.text:
MIRACLE-2: RT to primary/mets -> chemo + tislelizumab in MSS unresectable met rectal ca (N=50): 68% ORR & median OS 23 mo.

Early, single-arm data, but ~1 in 5 pts reached NED.

Suggests RT + systemic + PD1 blockade could overcome immune resistance in MSS mCRC. #ASCO26 @OncoAlert pic.twitter.com/sjnUW8x7f3
bookmarks.html:
MIRACLE-2: RT to primary/mets -> chemo + tislelizumab in MSS unresectable met rectal ca (N=50): 68% ORR & median OS 23 mo.

Early, single-arm data, but ~1 in 5 pts reached NED.

Suggests RT + systemic + PD1 blockade could overcome immune resistance in MSS mCRC. #ASCO26 @OncoAlert pic.twitter.com/sjnUW8x7f3

— Dr. Nina Niu Sanford (@NiuSanford) May 31, 2026
image_ocr_texts:
Abstract 539778: Radiotherapy followed by systemic therapy and Tislelizumab in microsatellite-stable rectal cancer
with synchronous unresectable metastases: Updated results of the MIRACLE-2 study
Menglong Zhou, 'Lijun Shen, 2Zezhi Shan, 'Hui Zhang, Wang Yang, 'Yajie Chen, "Yaqi Wang, 'Shujuan Zhou, 'Ruiyan Wu, Juefeng Wan,
2Dakui Luo, "Zilan Ye, 'Weijing He, "Yufei Yang, 'Yikuan Chen, "Changming Zhou, 'Qingguo Li, "Fan Xia, 'Xinxiang Li, 'Zhen Zhang
Department of Radiation Oncology, -Department of Colorectal Surgery, Department of Cancer Prevention, Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center, Shanghai, PR China
*Clinical Oncology School of Fujian Medical University, Fujian Cancer Hospital (Fujian Branch of Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center), Fuzhou, PR China.
Background
Microsatellite-stable (MSS) tumors comprise ~95% of
metastatic colorectal cancer cases and exhibit intrinsic
resistance to immunotherapy. Emerging evidence suggests
systemic therapy may enhance immunotherapy
responsiveness, while radiotherapy could improve efficacy by
overcoming resistance and reducing tumor burden. The
MIRACLE-2 study evaluated the safety and efficacy of
combining radiotherapy with systemic therapy and
Tislelizumab as first-line treatment for unresectable
metastatic MSS rectal cancer (RC).
Methods
MIRACLE-2 was a prospective, single-arm, phase I study.
Inclusion criteria: MSS RC with primary tumor ≤10 cm from
anal verge on MRI and synchronous unresectable
metastases. Patients received hypofractionated radiotherapy
(HFRT) for primary lesions and HFRT or SBRT for metastases,
followed by systemic therapy: FOLFOX-bevacizumab-
Tislelizumab for RAS/BRAF-mutant patients or FOLFIRI-
cetuximab-Tislelizumab for wild-type patients. Reassessment
every 8 weeks. Patients achieving resectable disease
underwent primary tumor resection and
metastasectomy/local ablative therapies for metastases. For
patients unable to preserve anal sphincter, a watch-and-wait
(WW) strategy was considered if clinical complete response
(cCR) of primary tumor was achieved. Otherwise, systemic
therapy continued until progression/intolerable toxicity.
Primary endpoint: ETS rate (≥20% target lesion reduction at 8
weeks post systemic therapy initiation). Secondary endpoints:
disease control rate (DCR), duration of response (DOR), overall
survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), and safety.
Results
As of December 31, 2025, efficacy data were available for 50 patients (38
males, 76.0%; median age 57 years, range 30-73). Among these, 52.0%
had liver metastases, 8.0% lung metastases, and 40.0% both liver and
lung metastases. RAS/BRAF mutations were detected in 56.0% of
primary tumors. Median treatment courses: eight (range 3-12). Overall,
18% (9/50) attained no evidence of disease (NED). ETS rate: 76.0%;
DCR: 88.0%. Median follow-up: 19.9 months (95% Cl: 16.4-23.4). Among
34 patients with complete/partial response, median DOR: 8.0 months
(95% Cl: 5.2-10.8), with 1-year DOR rate of 20%. Median PFS: 9.3 months
(95% Cl: 7.1-11.5), with 1-year PFS rate of 33.4%. Median OS: 23.2
months (95% Cl: 15.1-31.3), and 1-year OS rate: 93.3%. No grade 5
treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) occurred. All-grade TRAEs:
lymphopenia (95.9%), anemia (91.8%), and leukopenia (69.4%). Grade
3/4 TRAEs: lymphopenia (36.7%), neutropenia (26.5%), and leukopenia
(20.4%).
Conclusions
The combination of radiotherapy, systemic therapy, and Tislelizumab
showed a high ETS rate and manageable safety profile in first-line
unresectable MSS RC. Long-term outcomes require further follow-up.
Figure 1. The flowchart of Miracle-2 study

Future Directions for Research
Biomarker Discovery: There is a critical need to identify
predictive biomarkers that can select patients who are most
likely to respond to this trimodal treatment approach, moving
away from a "one-size-fits-all" strategy for MSS rectal cancer.
Acknowledgements
• We thank the FUSCC's Colorectal Cancer MDT for their
support. We also thank the study subjects for their
cooperation.
• Corresponding author: Prof. Zhen ZHANG

• PCSM:
Not significantly different (1 PCSM event in each arm)

ASCO =

22
Key Takeaway Points
Oncolytic-viral therapy in localized prostate cancer
Intra-prostatic CAN-2409 reduces relative clinical recurrence/death by
30%, driven by higher pathologic CR rates.
Understand role in context of ADT & modern RT dose intensification strategies
Digital-path based Al biomarker to personalize systemic therapy
Supports guiding abiraterone use in non-metastatic very high-risk
prostate cancer patients.
Role of ARPI in modern very high-risk unclear, await further prospective
validation

Figure 2. Overall Survival.
The RAS G12 population comprised patients with RAS G12 mutations. The overall population comprised patients whose tumors har-
bored RAS mutations at G12, G13, or Q61 or patients in whom no RAS mutation was identified. Hazard ratios and 95% confidence in-
tervals were based on the stratified Cox model with Efron's method of handling ties. P values were calculated with the stratified log-rank
test. NR denotes not reached.

bookmarks.text:
Thought experiment:

Let's take a very simple hypothetical trial involving 100 patients in the APA arm and 100 patients in the ADT alone arm. Pulling from PROTEUS EFS data, assume that, by 5 years, 60 patients in the ADT arm have had a BCR compared to 50 in the ADT+APA arm.… pic.twitter.com/WJaiDlJnQs

#ASCO26 
The PROTEUS trial results are now online...buckle up as we wait to see the full presentation. This is going to be a trial that is likely highly controversial until the full results are published.

Some may call this a homerun, others may call this the largest negative…
